question: UHV systems require special materials, surface preparation, and construction. Which combination of materials is the standard choice for a UHV chamber?

options: 316L stainless steel chamber; OFHC copper CF gaskets; aluminium-free borosilicate (Pyrex) or fused silica viewports; no Viton O-rings
6061 aluminium chamber for low outgassing; rubber O-rings backed by ion pump; brass viewports for ease of machining
Mild steel chamber; copper gaskets; soda-lime glass viewports; Viton O-rings everywhere for vacuum seals
PTFE-lined steel chamber; aluminium gaskets; PMMA viewports; silicone grease on every flange
passive.explanation: Outgassing rates at room temperature after bakeout: stainless steel $\sim 10^{-13}$ mbar·L/(s·cm$^2$); copper $\sim 10^{-13}$; PTFE (Teflon) $\sim 10^{-8}$ — 5 orders of magnitude higher, thus UHV-incompatible in bulk. Permitted polymers: PEEK, PTFE (sparingly), Kapton. Avoid: zinc, cadmium, all lubricants, tape, fingerprints. Every component must be degreased (acetone, IPA, ultrasonic), handled with clean-room gloves, and stored in vacuum or nitrogen.

active.prompt: Match each UHV hardware component to its purpose:

pairs.definition: Metal-to-metal seal via knife-edge biting into OFHC copper; UHV-rated; single use per gasket; leak rate $< 10^{-11}$ mbar·L/s
Gate valve with metal (Viton-free) seat for UHV isolation; 100% duty cycle; bakeable to 450°C
Maintains UHV after initial turbo pump-down; no vibration; can measure pressure from pump current; typical speed 25–300 L/s
Zr/V/Fe alloy activated at 350°C; pumps H$_2$, CO, CO$_2$, N$_2$, H$_2$O; no power after activation; ideal for small chambers

active.explanation: CF flanges are the standard UHV connection. The knife edge cuts into the copper gasket under bolt torque — cross-pattern torque, 3 rounds to full torque (typically 8–12 Nm for 2¾" CF). Never re-use copper gaskets. Silver-plated bolts prevent galling at bakeout temperatures. Electropolishing the inner surface of stainless steel reduces H$_2$ outgassing by $5{-}10\times$.

question: Outgassing is the release of dissolved or adsorbed gas from vacuum-chamber walls. After a proper bakeout, what is the dominant outgassing species at room temperature and what is its rate?

options: H$_2$ dominates ($> 90\%$ of residual gas); rate $\sim 10^{-13}$ mbar·L/(s·cm$^2$) after 24 h bakeout at 250°C; $Q_{\rm H_2} \propto 1/t$ time dependence
Water vapour dominates in stainless steel chambers even after a 24 h bakeout at 250°C
The outgassing rate is constant in time and independent of temperature for stainless steel
Baking at $> 400°$C reduces outgassing further, but only by making the steel porous and allowing trapped gas to escape
passive.explanation: Without bakeout: water (adsorbed on surfaces) dominates at $\sim 10^{-9}$ mbar·L/(s·cm$^2$). After bakeout at 200°C for 24 h: H$_2$ from bulk diffusion dominates at $\sim 10^{-12}$ mbar·L/(s·cm$^2$). The $Q \propto 1/t$ time dependence of H$_2$ outgassing (vs $1/\sqrt{t}$ for diffusion-limited) suggests the dominant mechanism is surface desorption. A 40 cm × 30 cm cylindrical chamber has $A \approx 3000$ cm$^2$; total outgassing $Q \approx 3\times 10^{-10}$ mbar·L/s, requiring $S > 300$ L/s for $p = 10^{-12}$ mbar.

question: A UHV system fails to reach base pressure. What is the standard technique for detecting a real leak (path to atmosphere)?

options: Spray helium around suspected leak points while monitoring an He-tuned mass spectrometer on the vacuum side; sensitivity $\sim 10^{-12}$ mbar·L/s
Squirt water around flanges and look for bubbles forming as the leak draws water in through the chamber wall
Spray acetone on suspected joints and watch for downward pressure spikes on the ion gauge
Increase the fore-line pressure deliberately and look for frequency changes in the turbomolecular pump
passive.explanation: Helium leak detector sensitivity: $\sim 10^{-12}$ mbar·L/s — can detect leaks far too small to cause measurable pressure rise. Procedure: connect a turbo-backed He leak detector via its own port or the turbo foreline. Spray He around each flange, feedthrough, viewport, and weld (3–5 second wait per location for He to diffuse to the leak). A positive signal means flow from that location. Virtual leaks (trapped gas pockets) appear as slow, sustained signals that do not respond to He spray — fix by adding venting holes to trapped volumes.

question: Atomic vapour dispensers (Rb, Cs) are commonly used to load MOTs in UHV chambers. Why are they preferred over breaking a glass ampule in vacuum?

options: Heated only when current applied — loading rate and background pressure independently controlled; small release per shot avoids spikes
Glass ampules cannot be manufactured to the chemical purity required for reliable MOT loading of cold atoms
Dispensers continuously release Rb vapour without power input, simplifying the wiring and control electronics
Glass ampules release far too little Rb vapour to load a working MOT within any reasonable experiment time
passive.explanation: SAES Getters (e.g., Rb/NF/3.4/12 FT10+10) contain Rb in a zeolite compound. At low current (3–5 A): slow release (MOT loading $\sim$ 5 s to steady state). At higher current: fast release, pressure spike. Typical operating current: 3.5–4.5 A. Dispenser lifetime: $\sim 10^{5}$ ampere-hours. When the dispenser is off, the Rb vapour pressure drops in $\sim 5$ s (gettered by NEG and ion pump). Light-induced atomic desorption (LIAD) using UV/blue light is an alternative for all-UHV operation.

question: A differential pumping stage connects two vacuum regions at very different pressures. What sets the achievable pressure ratio?

options: $p_1/p_2 \approx S_2/(C_{12} + S_1)$ where $C_{12}$ is the conductance of the connecting tube; long narrow tubes give ratios of $10^3{-}10^5$
Differential pumping can at most produce a $\sim 10\times$ pressure difference between the two chambers
The achievable pressure ratio equals the ratio of chamber volumes
Differential pumping works only for noble gases — reactive species can still equilibrate across the stage
passive.explanation: Differential pumping enables, e.g., a Zeeman slower connecting a hot oven at $10^{-8}$ mbar to a UHV science chamber at $10^{-11}$ mbar. The Zeeman slower tube (inner diameter $\sim 1$ cm, length $\sim 50$ cm) acts as the differential pumping aperture: $C \approx 3$ L/s. With the slower pumped by a 60 L/s ion pump at its midpoint: ratio $\sim 10^4$. Multiple differential pumping stages (e.g., pyramid MOT + science cell) achieve $p_{\rm science}/p_{\rm source} \sim 10^5{-}10^7$.
